html:
[622]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 其东有平原，若演武场 - Describes a plain to the east resembling a military training ground.
  • 有操弓矢者，有执旌旗者，骑而驰骤者，步而击刺者 - Describes people practicing archery, holding flags, riding horses, and practicing martial arts.
  • 有若大官鸣驺清道而至者 - Describes a high-ranking official arriving with a clear path, indicating a ceremonial procession.
  • 有若市井交易者，负者、戴者、坐者、趋者，亦历历可数也 - Describes a market scene with people carrying, wearing, sitting, and hurrying.
  • 有若城郭者，有若大船帆风而行者，莫能窥其际矣 - Describes distant city walls and large ships sailing on the wind.
  • 俄而一峰突起，有云气环绕之，殷殷然有雷声 - Describes a sudden mountain peak appearing with clouds and thunder.
  • 其中忽涌出七级浮屠，上插霄汉，铃铎动摇，光彩照耀，而其云气之变幻，莫能名状也 - Describes a seven-story pagoda emerging with bells and lights, and clouds changing shape.
  • 久之昏雾复起，海风亦作，所见之象，倏归乌有矣 - Describes the scene fading away with fog and sea wind.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 演武场 - A military training ground.
  • 大官鸣驺清道而至 - A high-ranking official arriving with a clear path, indicating a ceremonial procession.
  • 市井交易 - Market transactions, reflecting everyday life.
  • 城郭 - City walls, symbolizing urban civilization.
  • 大船帆风而行 - Large ships sailing on the wind, reflecting maritime activity.
  • 七级浮屠 - A seven-story pagoda, a symbol of Buddhist architecture.
  • 铃铎动摇，光彩照耀 - Bells and lights moving, reflecting the dynamic and vibrant atmosphere of the scene.
  • 云气之变幻，莫能名状 - Clouds changing shape, reflecting the mystical and transient nature of the scene.
  • 观海市记 - A record of the sea market, a literary work describing a unique natural phenomenon.
  • 震泽平望镇 - Pingwang Town, Zhenze, a place name.
  • 乾、嘉间 - The Qianlong and Jiaqing periods, referring to the reigns of Emperors Qianlong and Jiaqing.
  • 戊戌春 - The spring of the year of the earth rooster, a specific time period.

Proper Nouns

  • 翁君 - Mr. Wang, the author of the record.
  • 《观海市记》 - The title of the record.
  • 暴梅村 - Mr. Bao Meicun, a historical figure.
  • 式昭字方子 - Mr. Fang, a contemporary of the author.
  • 震泽平望镇 - Pingwang Town, Zhenze, a place name.
  • 乾、嘉间 - The Qianlong and Jiaqing periods.
  • 戊戌春 - The spring of the year of the earth rooster.

[623]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 西湖南高峰之下有五老峰 - Literally means 'Under the South Peak of West Lake, there is the Five Elders Peak.'
  • 俗呼謂老虎洞 - '俗呼' means 'commonly called' or 'popularly known as,' and '老虎洞' (Tiger's Den) is a colloquial name for the location.
  • 然亦罕有遇之者 - '然亦' means 'yet also,' and '罕有遇之者' means 'rarely encountered by people.'
  • 道光某年 - '道光' is the era name of Emperor Daoguang of the Qing Dynasty, and '某年' means 'a certain year.'
  • 高氏于其地营葬 - '高氏' refers to a family or individual named Gao, and '营葬' means 'to prepare a grave.'
  • 百杵俱举，邪许相闻 - '百杵俱举' describes a loud noise from many hammers, and '邪许相闻' means 'echoing sounds.'
  • 俄闻虎啸声甚厉 - '俄' means 'soon,' and '虎啸声甚厉' describes a loud and fierce tiger roar.
  • 众皆奔走恐后，工遂辍 - '众皆奔走恐后' means 'everyone ran in fear,' and '工遂辍' means 'the work was then stopped.'
  • 高氏患之，募求能得虎者 - '高氏患之' means 'the Gao family was troubled by it,' and '募求能得虎者' means 'they sought someone who could catch the tiger.'
  • 其附近有翁家山，山中人多膂力 - '翁家山' is a place name, and '山中人多膂力' means 'the people in the mountain have strong bodies.'
  • 先用一木盆，置大铜镜于其中，烈日照映，光焰上腾，若火起然 - '先用一木盆' means 'first, they used a wooden basin,' '置大铜镜于其中' means 'placed a large copper mirror inside,' '烈日照映' means 'intense sunlight reflected,' '光焰上腾' means 'flames rose upward,' and '若火起然' means 'as if a fire had started.'
  • 于是山中之蜂飞集以万计，咸遗溺于盆中 - '于是' means 'thus,' '山中之蜂飞集以万计' means 'bees from the mountain flew and gathered in countless numbers,' and '咸遗溺于盆中' means 'all left their urine in the basin.'
  • 土人言 - '土人' means 'local people' or 'rustics.'
  • 蜂见火起辄来救，遗溺所以救火也 - '蜂见火起辄来救' means 'bees see a fire start and come to help,' and '遗溺所以救火也' means 'they leave their urine to extinguish the fire.'
  • 如是数日，积溺盈寸 - '如是数日' means 'for several days,' and '积溺盈寸' means 'the urine accumulated to an inch.'
  • 乃捉一虾蟆，以溺少许著其身上 - '乃' means 'then,' '捉一虾蟆' means 'they caught a frog,' '以溺少许著其身上' means 'and placed a small amount of urine on its body.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 西湖南高峰 - Refers to a specific location near West Lake in Hangzhou, a famous scenic area in China.
  • 五老峰 - A mountain peak with a cultural or mythological significance, possibly named after five elders or sages.
  • 老虎洞 - A colloquial name for a location, likely associated with a legend or local folklore.
  • 道光 - The era name of Emperor Daoguang (r. 1820-1850), a period in the Qing Dynasty.
  • 高氏 - A family or individual named Gao, possibly a local landowner or noble.
  • 翁家山 - A place name, likely a mountain or village known for its strong and capable inhabitants.

Proper Nouns

  • 西湖南高峰 - A specific location near West Lake in Hangzhou.
  • 五老峰 - A mountain peak with a cultural or mythological significance.
  • 老虎洞 - A colloquial name for a location.
  • 道光 - The era name of Emperor Daoguang.
  • 高氏 - A family or individual named Gao.
  • 翁家山 - A place name, likely a mountain or village.

[624]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 中其毒 - Literally 'the toad is poisoned,' but in this context, it refers to the toad being affected by the poison.
  • 跳一步而死 - Means 'jumps one step and dies,' describing the immediate effect of the poison.
  • 亦跳一步而死 - Means 'also jumps one step and dies,' indicating that the tiger, like the toad, dies after jumping one step.
  • 亦跳数步死 - Means 'also jumps several steps and dies,' indicating that both the toad and the tiger die after jumping several steps.
  • 率不爽 - Means 'always without fail,' indicating that the effect is consistent.
  • 伏巨弩 - Means 'set up a large crossbow,' referring to the preparation of a weapon.
  • 弩末以蜂溺涂之 - Means 'the tip of the crossbow is coated with bee venom,' describing the method of poisoning.
  • 一人升树杪观动静 - Means 'one person climbs to the top of a tree to observe the movements,' describing the surveillance setup.
  • 又数十人执火枪伏山下 - Means 'and dozens of others hold fire guns and lie in wait at the foot of the mountain,' describing the additional preparations.
  • 闻山上爆竹声 - Means 'when they hear the sound of firecrackers on the mountain,' indicating the signal for action.
  • 奔赴 - Means 'rush to the scene,' describing the response to the signal.
  • 是日虎不出 - Means 'that day, the tiger did not come out,' indicating the tiger's absence.
  • 次日闻虎啸声 - Means 'the next day, they heard the tiger's roar,' indicating the tiger's return.
  • 有一伥鬼先出 - Means 'a shanggui (tiger's servant) came out first,' describing the appearance of the tiger's companion.
  • 此不利于大哥 - Means 'this is not good for the big brother,' indicating the shanggui's warning to the tiger.
  • 移置他所 - Means 'move it to another place,' describing the shanggui's action to avoid the trap.
  • 树上人俟其去远 - Means 'the person on the tree waits until it goes far away,' describing the timing of the trap reset.
  • 下树置弩如故 - Means 'climbs down the tree and sets the crossbow back in place,' describing the reset of the trap.
  • 复升树 - Means 'climbs back up the tree,' describing the return to surveillance.
  • 触机弩发 - Means 'the trigger of the crossbow is activated,' describing the activation of the trap.
  • 中其毒死 - Means 'is poisoned and dies,' describing the tiger's death.
  • 仰视有人在树杪 - Means 'looks up and sees someone at the top of the tree,' describing the shanggui's reaction.
  • 踊跃欲上 - Means 'jumps up eagerly,' describing the shanggui's attempt to climb the tree.
  • 击石取火然爆竹 - Means 'strikes a stone to light a fire and sets off firecrackers,' describing the signal for the ambush.
  • 响振山谷 - Means 'the sound reverberates through the valley,' describing the effect of the firecrackers.
  • 山下所伏人皆上 - Means 'all the people lying in wait at the foot of the mountain rush up,' describing the response to the signal.
  • 咸发火枪 - Means 'all fire their guns,' describing the coordinated attack.
  • 逃去 - Means 'flees away,' describing the shanggui's escape.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 虾蟆 - Refers to a toad, a common animal in Chinese folklore.
  • 虎 - Refers to a tiger, a powerful and feared animal in Chinese culture.
  • 伥鬼 - Refers to a shanggui, a spirit that serves as a tiger's companion and is often depicted as a ghost in Chinese folklore.
  • 弩 - Refers to a crossbow, a traditional weapon in Chinese history.
  • 蜂溺 - Refers to bee venom, a substance known for its toxicity.
  • 火枪 - Refers to early firearms, which were used in Chinese military and hunting contexts.
  • 爆竹 - Refers to firecrackers, a traditional Chinese firework used for various purposes, including signaling and celebration.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence compares the immediate effects of the poison on the toad and the tiger.
  • This sentence extends the comparison to show that both the toad and the tiger die after jumping several steps, and this effect is always consistent.
  • This sentence describes the setup of the trap, with the crossbow placed where the tiger enters and exits, and the tip coated with bee venom.
  • This sentence describes the surveillance and ambush setup, with one person on the tree and dozens of others in wait at the foot of the mountain.
  • This sentence describes the sequence of events leading up to the tiger's capture, including the shanggui's warning and action to avoid the trap.
  • This sentence describes the reset of the trap after the shanggui's departure.
  • This sentence describes the activation of the trap and the tiger's death.
  • This sentence describes the shanggui's reaction and the signal for the ambush.
  • This sentence describes the coordinated attack and the shanggui's escape.

[625]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 荷虎以归 - Metaphorical expression meaning to shoulder a heavy burden or responsibility.
  • 厚赉之 - Means 'rewarded him generously,' indicating a significant reward.
  • 就馆于宣化府 - Means 'taught in the Xuanhua Prefecture,' indicating his place of work.
  • 偶小病月余 - Means 'suffered a minor illness for over a month,' describing his health condition.
  • 请速赴任 - Means 'please hurry to your post,' a command or request to resume duty.
  • 肩舆已俟于门 - Means 'a sedan chair was waiting at the door,' indicating preparation for travel.
  • 荷之者四人 - Means 'four people carrying it,' referring to the bearers of the sedan chair.
  • 旗伞之属 - Means 'flags and umbrellas and the like,' indicating ceremonial items.
  • 似人间衙署 - Means 'like a human government office,' describing the appearance of the place.
  • 门塾堂庑规模略具 - Means 'the layout of the gatehouse, study, hall, and corridors was roughly present,' describing the structure.
  • 颇隘小 - Means 'quite narrow and small,' indicating the size of the place.
  • 堂上设公案，置印其上 - Means 'a public desk was set up on the hall, with an official seal placed on it,' describing the setup.
  • 赞拜如仪 - Means 'paid homage according to the ritual,' indicating the formal process.
  • 遂登公坐 - Means 'then ascended to the official seat,' indicating his position.
  • 吏役参拜 - Means 'officials and servants paid homage,' describing the attendees.
  • 按籍呼名 - Means 'called names according to the register,' indicating the process of calling names.
  • 点以朱笔 - Means 'marked with red ink,' indicating the act of marking.
  • 事已退入内 - Means 'after the matter was settled, he withdrew inside,' indicating the conclusion of the event.
  • 则小屋三间 - Means 'then there were three small rooms,' describing the interior.
  • 有二仆为之执役 - Means 'there were two servants who performed the duties,' indicating the staff.
  • 须臾具饭 - Means 'soon the meal was prepared,' indicating the timing.
  • 馔以四簋 - Means 'the meal consisted of four bowls,' describing the food.
  • 切猪肉作丝 - Means 'sliced pork into threads,' describing the preparation of pork.
  • 蒸鸡卵作饼 - Means 'steamed chicken eggs into cakes,' describing the preparation of chicken eggs.
  • 余则蔬菜 - Means 'the rest were vegetables,' indicating the other components of the meal.
  • 其味悉如人间 - Means 'the taste was all like that of the human world,' indicating the quality of the food.
  • 饭饱之后 - Means 'after the meal,' indicating the time frame.
  • 一无所事 - Means 'nothing to do,' indicating the lack of activity.
  • 既无公牍披阅 - Means 'there were no official documents to read,' indicating the absence of work.
  • 亦无吏胥禀白 - Means 'nor were there any officials reporting,' indicating the absence of communication.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 荷虎以归 - A metaphorical expression that can be traced back to ancient Chinese literature, often used to describe a heavy burden or responsibility.
  • 厚赉之 - The practice of rewarding officials or scholars was common in ancient China, reflecting the importance of merit and service.
  • 就馆于宣化府 - Xuanhua Prefecture was an important administrative region in ancient China, and teaching in such a place indicates a respected position.
  • 偶小病月余 - Minor illnesses were common and could affect daily activities, especially for officials and scholars.
  • 请速赴任 - The urgency to resume duty reflects the importance of official responsibilities and the expectation of prompt action.
  • 肩舆已俟于门 - Sedan chairs were a common mode of transportation for officials and scholars, indicating a prepared and organized response.
  • 旗伞之属 - Ceremonial items like flags and umbrellas were used in official processions and rituals, symbolizing authority and status.
  • 似人间衙署 - Government offices were central to the administration of ancient Chinese society, and their appearance and function were well-known.
  • 门塾堂庑规模略具 - The layout of government offices followed a standard design, with specific rooms and corridors for different functions.
  • 颇隘小 - The size of the place might reflect the simplicity or modesty of the setting, contrasting with the grandeur of larger official buildings.
  • 堂上设公案，置印其上 - The setup of a public desk with an official seal was a standard feature of government offices, symbolizing authority and order.
  • 赞拜如仪 - Rituals and ceremonies were an important part of ancient Chinese culture, and following them was a sign of respect and propriety.
  • 遂登公坐 - Ascending to an official seat was a formal act that indicated a person's rank and authority.
  • 吏役参拜 - Officials and servants paying homage was a common practice in government offices, reflecting the hierarchical nature of the society.
  • 按籍呼名 - Calling names according to a register was a method used to manage and organize people, often in official contexts.
  • 点以朱笔 - Marking with red ink was a common practice in ancient Chinese administration, used to indicate approval or confirmation.
  • 事已退入内 - Withdrawing inside after completing a task was a formal act, indicating the conclusion of a meeting or event.
  • 则小屋三间 - Three small rooms might be a simple living or working space, reflecting the modesty of the setting.
  • 有二仆为之执役 - Having two servants indicates a certain level of status and the ability to support a household.
  • 须臾具饭 - Preparing a meal quickly suggests efficiency and hospitality.
  • 馔以四簋 - Four bowls of food was a standard serving size in ancient Chinese meals, indicating a balanced and complete meal.
  • 切猪肉作丝 - Slicing pork into threads was a common culinary technique, often used to enhance the texture and flavor of the dish.
  • 蒸鸡卵作饼 - Steaming chicken eggs into cakes was a unique preparation method, combining different ingredients to create a special dish.
  • 余则蔬菜 - Vegetables were a staple part of the diet, providing essential nutrients and variety.
  • 其味悉如人间 - The taste being like that of the human world suggests that the food was familiar and satisfying.
  • 饭饱之后 - After the meal, the focus shifts to the lack of activity and the peaceful state of mind.
  • 一无所事 - The absence of work or responsibilities allows for reflection and relaxation.
  • 既无公牍披阅 - The lack of official documents to read indicates a period of rest or downtime.
  • 亦无吏胥禀白 - The absence of reports from officials suggests a quiet and uneventful period.

[626]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 视其印文 - To look at the text of the seal or examining the seal's inscription.
  • 甫合眼，蘧然觉矣 - As soon as he closed his eyes, he suddenly woke up, where '蘧然' is an adverb indicating a sudden awakening.
  • 服之，则不来矣 - If you take them, you won't come here anymore.
  • 吞之，忽如梦醒 - He swallowed them, and suddenly it was as if he woke up from a dream.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 八达岭 - A section of the Great Wall of China, known for its strategic importance and scenic beauty.
  • 宣化郡斋 - The study or office of Xuanhua Prefecture, indicating a local government office.
  • 巡检 - A low-ranking official responsible for local security and law enforcement.
  • 直隶 - A province in northern China, historically significant for its proximity to the imperial capital.
  • 旋风 - A whirlwind, often associated with supernatural or unusual occurrences in classical literature.
  • 故吏 - Former officials or servants, implying a past connection or service.
  • 迎候 - To greet or welcome, often used in the context of official duties.

Proper Nouns

  • 汪 - The protagonist, Wang, a low-ranking official.
  • 八达岭 - A specific location on the Great Wall of China.
  • 宣化郡斋 - The study or office in Xuanhua Prefecture.
  • 直隶 - A historical province in northern China.
  • 旋风 - A whirlwind, often associated with supernatural phenomena.
  • 故吏 - Former officials or servants.

[627]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 年且五十矣 - Nearly fifty years old; '且' indicates approximation.
  • 与比邻徐叟通 - Had an affair with her neighbor, Mr. Xu; '通' refers to an extramarital relationship.
  • 不忍弃 - Could not bear to abandon; conveys a sense of emotional reluctance.
  • 早寡 - Became a widow at a young age.
  • 使女子之 - Sent her daughter to take care of him; somewhat elliptical, implying the daughter was sent to care for the son.
  • 托言得之育婴堂者 - Claimed that she had obtained him from a nursery; '托言' means to claim or pretend.
  • 如所出 - Treated him as if he were her own child; '如所出' means 'as if he were her own.'
  • 性甚悍 - Nature was very fierce; '性甚悍' describes a strong, aggressive character.
  • 恒操刀与母斗 - Often wielded a knife and fought with his mother; '恒操刀' means 'often wielded a knife.'
  • 患之 - Was troubled by this; '患之' means 'was troubled by it.'
  • 适至 - Just arrived; '适至' means 'just arrived.'
  • 养虎畜狼 - Raising a tiger and a wolf; metaphor for raising a dangerous or uncontrollable child.
  • 逐之 - Send him away; '逐之' means 'send him away.'
  • 与辩 - Argued with her; '与辩' means 'argued with her.'
  • 忽大声谓 - Suddenly shouted at; '忽大声谓' means 'suddenly shouted at.'
  • 何得子我 - How can you treat me as your own child; '何得子我' means 'how can you treat me as your own child.'
  • 失色 - Turned pale; '失色' means 'turned pale.'
  • 骇异 - Surprised; '骇异' means 'surprised.'
  • 细诘之 - Questioned him in detail; '细诘之' means 'questioned him in detail.'
  • 穷究 - Investigate further; '穷究' means 'investigate further.'
  • 复不语矣 - Fell silent again; '复不语矣' means 'fell silent again.'
  • 年益长，性益暴 - As he grew older, his nature became even more violent; '益长' and '益暴' indicate increasing age and increasing violence.
  • 合而同居 - Combined and lived together; '合而同居' means 'combined and lived together.'
  • 受之不辞 - Accepted him without hesitation; '受之不辞' means 'accepted him without hesitation.'
  • 遂能言 - Suddenly began to speak; '遂能言' means 'suddenly began to speak.'
  • 尚为木匠云 - Is still a carpenter; '尚为木匠云' means 'is still a carpenter.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 绍兴 - Shaoxing, a city in Zhejiang Province, China, known for its historical significance and cultural heritage.
  • 嫠 - A widow, a term used to describe a woman who has lost her husband.
  • 比邻 - A neighbor, indicating a close living proximity.
  • 徐叟 - Mr. Xu, an elderly man, likely a neighbor or acquaintance.
  • 育婴堂 - A nursery or orphanage, where children are cared for.
  • 夫族 - The husband's relatives, indicating a family connection.
  • 养虎畜狼 - A metaphor for raising a dangerous or uncontrollable child.
  • 木匠 - A carpenter, a skilled worker who makes and repairs wooden structures.

[628]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 异之 - Means 'found it strange' or 'was surprised by it.'
  • 觇焉 - Means 'peeked in' or 'looked inside.'
  • 神坐堂上 - Means 'the god sits on the dais.'
  • 吏役环侍 - Means 'officials and servants surround and attend.'
  • 按籍而呼名者 - Means 'those who call names according to a register.'
  • 阶下鹄立之人 - Means 'people standing like swans on the steps,' a metaphor for standing respectfully.
  • 鱼贯而入 - Means 'entering one after another like fish in a line.'
  • 列坐东西 - Means 'seated in rows on both sides.'
  • 阑入其内 - Means 'slipped in secretly' or 'entered without permission.'
  • 循墙行 - Means 'walk along the wall.'
  • 署曰“受卷所” - Means 'a sign indicating 'the place to receive scrolls.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 杭州 - Refers to Hangzhou, a significant city in Zhejiang Province, known for its cultural and historical importance.
  • 清泰门 - Refers to the Qingtai Gate, one of the gates in ancient Hangzhou.
  • 士人 - Refers to scholars or literati, who were highly respected in Chinese society.
  • 社庙 - Refers to a local temple dedicated to community deities or ancestral spirits.
  • 神 - Refers to a deity or spirit, often associated with local beliefs and practices.
  • 吏役 - Refers to officials and servants, typically associated with the temple or local administration.
  • 亡友 - Refers to a deceased friend, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 杭州 - Hangzhou, a city in Zhejiang Province.
  • 清泰门 - Qingtai Gate, a gate in ancient Hangzhou.
  • 徐彭宣 - Xu Pengxuan, the name of the protagonist.
  • 曹某 - Cao, the name of the deceased friend.

[629]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 自写履历 - Literal expression meaning 'to write one's own resume' or 'to record one's own biography,' but in this context, it refers to a spiritual process of self-examination.
  • 生平行事 - Means 'lifetime actions' or 'all one's deeds.'
  • 为善为恶 - Means 'doing good or evil,' indicating a moral evaluation of actions.
  • 无所讳饰 - Means 'without any concealment or embellishment,' suggesting full honesty.
  • 尚自知其过 - Means 'still knows one's own faults,' implying self-awareness of wrongdoing.
  • 转送冥司科罪 - Means 'to be sent to the underworld for punishment,' referring to divine retribution in the afterlife.
  • 不能生还矣 - Means 'cannot return to life,' indicating a final judgment.
  • 及此不出，即不能出矣 - Means 'if you don't leave now, you won't be able to leave,' warning of imminent danger.
  • 履阈而仆 - Means 'to fall upon stepping on the threshold,' describing a sudden collapse.
  • 身卧庙庑 - Means 'lying in the temple porch,' indicating a physical state after a spiritual experience.
  • 微以所见隐事叩之 - Means 'gently probing with the hidden matters I saw,' referring to subtle questioning.
  • 大骇 - Means 'greatly startled,' indicating a strong emotional reaction.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '或五日或十日不归其家' indicates a variable duration of absence, which can be read as either 'sometimes for five days, sometimes for ten days' or 'for five or ten days without returning home.'
  • The structure '归而问之，则曰' introduces a direct quote, which can be read as 'returning and asking about it, he says' or 'returning and asking about it, he is told.'
  • The sequence '有僧招之去，从某山，过某山，至某山' can be read as a continuous journey or a series of distinct stops.
  • The phrase '率皆高山峻岭' emphasizes the general nature of the terrain, which can be read as 'generally all are high and steep mountains' or 'most of them are high and steep mountains.'
  • The clause '其所至之某山，则必素有神庙者也' links the destination to the presence of a shrine, which can be read as 'the mountain he arrives at, which has always had a shrine' or 'the mountain he arrives at, which must have always had a shrine.'
  • The phrase '一日必行千余里，盖飞行于空中也' describes the speed and mode of travel, which can be read as 'one day must travel thousands of miles, it is flying in the air' or 'one day travels thousands of miles, it is flying in the air.'
  • The phrase '俗云此天狗所为' introduces a popular belief, which can be read as 'commonly said to be the work of tian gou' or 'it is commonly believed to be the work of tian gou.'
  • The phrase '彼中相传多在深山穷谷' describes the habitat, which can be read as 'it is said to be mostly in deep mountains and remote valleys' or 'it is believed to be mostly in deep mountains and remote valleys.'
  • The phrase '古神佛之灵场' describes sacred sites, which can be read as 'ancient sites of gods and Buddhas' or 'ancient sites sacred to gods and Buddhas.'
  • The description '其形如人，衣僧衣，执羽扇，鼻准高七八寸，背有翅' can be read as 'its shape is like a human, wearing monk's robes, holding a feather fan, with a nose and bridge seven or eight inches high, with wings on the back' or 'it looks like a human, wears monk's robes, holds a feather fan, has a nose and bridge seven or eight inches high, and has wings on the back.'
  • The description '木叶天狗者，觜如鸟，手足皆四爪，背亦有翅' can be read as 'the wood leaf tian gou, with a beak like a bird, with four claws on hands and feet, with wings on the back' or 'the wood leaf tian gou, has a beak like a bird, has four claws on hands and feet, and has wings on the back.'
  • The phrase '赏善罚恶，其所司也' describes the function, which can be read as 'rewarding good and punishing evil, it is its duty' or 'it rewards good and punishes evil, which is its duty.'
  • The phrase '不知何以呼为天狗' expresses uncertainty, which can be read as 'not knowing why it is called tian gou' or 'not knowing why it is referred to as tian gou.'
  • The phrase '而是时且为人祟' indicates a negative effect, which can be read as 'but at that time it is a nuisance to people' or 'but at that time it is a disturbance to people.'
  • The phrase '不知其何理也' expresses confusion, which can be read as 'not knowing what the reason is' or 'not understanding the reason.'
  • The phrase '彼国倘有《五行志》' refers to a possible record, which can be read as 'if that country has a 'Wuxing Zhi'' or 'if that country has a 'Five Elements Record.'
  • The phrase '此等事在所必载矣' indicates the likelihood of documentation, which can be read as 'these kinds of things must be recorded' or 'these kinds of things will certainly be recorded.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '与大儿妇母家樊氏有连' is somewhat ambiguous, but it likely means 'has a connection with the family of his eldest son's mother, the Fan family.'
  • The conditional statement '若洗足，则无救' can be read as 'if one washes their feet, there is no hope of recovery.'
  • The sentence structure '沈欲洗足，家人使人招樊君萼楼至' is somewhat elliptical, but it means 'Shen Qiu Feng wanted to wash his feet, so the family sent someone to invite Fan Jue Lou.'
  • The phrase '自日失至于丙夜' is somewhat ambiguous, but it likely means 'from dusk until late at night.'
  • The phrase '其明日' can be read as 'the next day' or 'on that day.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • Refers to reports of strange or supernatural phenomena, which were common in ancient Chinese literature and folklore.
  • Implies that many of these reports were exaggerated or false, reflecting the skepticism of the author towards such tales.
  • Reflects a cultural value in ancient China that emphasized rationality and practicality.
  • Indicates that some reports, despite their strangeness, could still be logically explained or accepted.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 覩怪之說，類多妄誕，不近人情 - This sentence is a compound statement that lists multiple characteristics of the strange sightings.
  • 有合事理，不可不察 - This clause provides a nuanced view, acknowledging that some reports might be reasonable but still require careful examination.
  • 勿以傳聞為信 - This imperative statement advises against blindly believing in hearsay.

  • 顧女若無睹者 - Means 'looked at the girl as if he hadn’t seen her,' indicating the thief’s initial disregard for her.

  • 戲拍其肩 - Means 'playfully patted her shoulder,' a casual and disrespectful action.
  • 安得無禮，謂吾刃不利乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'How dare you be so rude? Do you think my blade is dull?' The girl is challenging the thief’s bravado.
  • 試獻所長，或可免爾死，不然血吾刃矣 - Means 'Show me your skills, and you might escape death; otherwise, I’ll make your blade wet with blood,' the girl issuing a challenge.
  • 盜聞大駭，乃曰 - Means 'The thief was greatly frightened and said,' indicating his reaction to the girl’s threat.
  • 請觀吾技 - Means 'Let me show you my skills,' the thief’s attempt to appease the girl.
  • 即向壁間攝衣而上，若有階級者 - Means 'He immediately climbed the wall, as if there were steps,' describing the thief’s acrobatic ability.
  • 及屋笮始卻行而下 - Means 'When he reached the eaves, he began to climb down,' describing the thief’s descent.
  • 技止此耳 - Means 'That’s all you’ve got,' the girl’s dismissive response.
  • 女舉纖趾蹋壁直上，及笮則翩然翻身以背負壁徐徐走下 - Means 'The girl lifted her slender foot, climbed the wall, reached the eaves, and gracefully flipped backward to descend,' describing her own acrobatic feat.

  • 食我珍羞，衣我文绮 - Describes being fed delicacies and dressed in fine silk. '珍羞' means 'delicacies,' and '文绮' means 'embroidered silk.'

  • 此间不乐欤？尚何归 - A rhetorical question asking if the person is not happy there and why they want to leave. '此间不乐欤' means 'Are you not happy here?' and '尚何归' means 'why do you want to leave?'
  • 然则今日何以得归？ - A question asking how the person was able to return today. '然则' means 'then' or 'therefore,' and '何以得归' means 'how did you manage to return.'

  • 畏罪仰药死 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'died by taking poison out of fear of punishment.' Refers to the act of taking poison as a form of suicide.

  • 我识其一人，顺义县城隍沈叔和也 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'I recognized one of them, the city god of Shunyi County, Shen Shuhe.' Refers to the recognition of a specific city god.

  • 莊愍與之並坐，殆與有香火因緣欤 - A rhetorical question suggesting a spiritual connection between Xu Zhuangmin and Yao.
  • 何子貞前輩紹基 - Refers to He Zizhen (何紹基), a renowned scholar and calligrapher.
  • 少年時夢至一處 - Describes He's dream when he was young.

  • 忽有人攘臂奪去 - Describes a sudden interruption where someone forcefully takes the steamed bun away.

  • 及嘉慶庚辰歲，桂林陳蓮史先生以己卯解元中式第一名貢士，遂魁天下 - Describes the events of the year 1810 (嘉慶庚辰), where Chen Lian Shi (陳蓮史) became the top scholar in the imperial examination.

  • 怅然曰 - Describes He's emotional reaction.
  • 吾其不能與此人爭乎 - A rhetorical question expressing He's doubt about competing with Chen.
  • 至道光乙未歲，子貞以第一人領解，而次年成進士，則會、狀皆非元也 - Describes the events of the year 1835 (道光乙未), where He became the top scholar in the provincial examination but did not win the top position in the next year's imperial examination.

  • 吾无子，止此一女 - Means 'I have no sons, only this one daughter,' with '止' meaning only or merely.
  • 不幸而盲，是无女也 - Means 'unfortunately, if she becomes blind, it’s as if I have no daughter,' with '是无女也' meaning it’s as if I have no daughter.
  • 二子但知自为计，君幸为我计 - Means 'the two sons only know how to think of themselves, please think of me,' with '自为计' meaning to think of oneself and '为我计' meaning to think of me.
  • 刘纳其言 - Means 'Liu accepted her words,' with '纳' meaning to accept.
  • 乃不与二子谋 - Means 'he then did not consult with the two sons,' with '谋' meaning to consult or plan.
  • 故使二子他适 - Means 'he deliberately sent the two sons elsewhere,' with '他适' meaning to send elsewhere.
  • 即发祖坟 - Means 'he immediately exhumed the ancestral grave,' with '发' meaning to exhume.
  • 甫起其棺，而女在家大叫，登时目盲 - Means 'as soon as the coffin was opened, the daughter at home screamed and immediately became blind,' with '甫' meaning as soon as and '登时' meaning immediately.
  • 迁葬之后，刘不久死，二子亦相继死，遂无后 - Means 'after the reburial, Liu did not live long, and the two sons also died one after another, leaving no descendants,' with '相继' meaning one after another.
  • 是古人固有改葬之礼 - Means 'it is clear that ancient people had the practice of reburial,' with '固有' meaning certainly had.
  • 然郑康成注曰：谓坟墓以他故崩坏，将亡失尸柩也 - Means 'but Zheng Xuan commented, 'it refers to graves that have collapsed due to other reasons, fearing the loss of the coffin and remains,' with '谓' meaning to say or refer to.
  • 是古人改葬，非如后世之以风水起见 - Means 'thus, ancient reburial was not like the later practice of considering feng shui,' with '起见' meaning to consider or take into account.
  • 惟唐李阳冰《扌西先茔记》 - Means 'only in the *Xianying Ji* (Record of the Ancestral Grave) by Li Yangbing of the Tang Dynasty,' with '惟' meaning only or merely.
  • 则实用方士邵权之说 - Means 'it actually used the words of the Taoist scholar Shao Quan,' with '实用' meaning actually used.

  • 盖唐时已有此等谬说矣 - Means 'it seems that such false beliefs already existed in the Tang Dynasty,' with '盖' meaning it seems or it appears.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 置火于中 - Literal phrase meaning 'place fire in the center,' used to set up the experiment.
  • 俄而 - Temporal adverb meaning 'shortly after' or 'soon,' indicating a brief period of time.
  • 薪化为火 - Literal phrase meaning 'the wood turns into fire,' describing the initial transformation.
  • 人生始化曰魄 - Classical phrase from the *Huangdi Neijing* meaning 'the beginning of life is called魄 (pò),' referring to the initial stage of life.
  • 既生魄，阳曰魂 - Classical phrase from the *Huangdi Neijing* meaning 'once魄 is formed, the yang aspect is called魂 (hún),' referring to the next stage of life.
  • 魂升而魄降 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the soul ascends while the body descends,' describing the process of death.
  • 烟上散而煤下坠 - Literal phrase meaning 'the smoke disperses upward while the coal falls downward,' used to illustrate the natural process of a flame extinguishing.
  • 魂已去，而其魄犹存 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the soul has departed, but the body remains,' describing the state of the woman.
  • 光已灭而火未绝 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the light has gone out, but the fire is not completely extinguished,' used to compare the woman's state to a partially extinguished flame.
  • 因记姚氏掘遗魄事 - Literal phrase meaning 'therefore, I recorded the story of Yao's excavation of the remaining body,' referring to a specific historical event.
  • 且说魂魄之义如此 - Literal phrase meaning 'and thus, the meaning of soul and body is as follows,' summarizing the explanation.
  • 众生必死，死必归土 - Classical phrase from the *Analects* of Confucius meaning 'all living things must die, and upon death, they return to the earth,' referring to the concept of death and the afterlife.
  • 其气发扬于上为昭明 - Literal phrase meaning 'its qi (vital energy) rises above and becomes bright and clear,' describing the spiritual aspect of a person.
  • 蒿凄怆 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'sad and mournful,' describing the emotional state associated with the spirit.
  • 百物之精也，神之著也 - Literal phrase meaning 'it is the essence of all things, the manifestation of the spirit,' referring to the spiritual nature of life.
  • 魂升魄降，姑以生时之名名之 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the soul ascends and the body descends, temporarily using the names of life,' explaining the temporary nature of the terms 'soul' and 'body.'
  • 其实，魂者火之光也，死而升者止其烟也；魄者火之所以为火也，死而降者止其煤也 - Literal phrase meaning 'in reality, the soul is the light of the fire, and what rises after death is the smoke; the body is the essence of the fire, and what falls after death is the coal,' providing a detailed explanation of the metaphor.
  • 观姚氏所掘其形如炭 - Literal phrase meaning 'observing the shape of what Yao excavated, it resembled charcoal,' providing evidence for the explanation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 姚氏掘遗魄事 - Refers to a specific historical event where Yao excavated a partially decomposed body, which is used to support the explanation of the soul and body.
  • 孔子 - Refers to Confucius, the founder of Confucianism, whose teachings are referenced in the passage.
  • 百物之精也，神之著也 - Refers to the spiritual nature of life, a concept deeply rooted in Chinese philosophy and religion.

Proper Nouns

  • 孔子 - Confucius.
  • 姚氏 - Yao, a historical figure who excavated a partially decomposed body.
